text: The surgeon retracts the colon and omentum superiorly, exposing the surgical area for the jejunostomy procedure.

The surgeon visually identifies the area of the duodenal jejunal flexure, ensuring clear visualization of the flexure and the inferior mesenteric vein to confirm the anatomical location, while checking for any adhesions.
The surgeon exposes the bowel, revealing the duodenal jejunal flexure clearly. The inferior mesenteric vein and artery are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon assesses the bowel to determine which segment can be freely brought to the anterior wall of the stomach for the jejunostomy procedure.
The surgeon observes the dilated stomach, which is visibly affected by gastric outlet obstruction, identifying the area for the planned anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make a gastrotomy incision in the thick stomach wall, preparing for the jejunostomy procedure.

The surgeon widens the gastrotomy using medallion forceps, followed by suctioning the residue inside the stomach. The duodenal jejunal flexure is identified, and the jejunum is brought up for further manipulation.
The surgeon visually confirms the absence of adhesions as the duodenal jejunal flexure is easily mobilized and brought into view.
The surgeon opens the mesentery of the jejunum and elevates it towards the anterior wall of the stomach, preparing the site for the anastomosis.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy in the jejunum using a monopolar hook, then widens the opening with Maryland forceps, preparing the site for anastomosis.
The mucosa is visible, and the surgeon introduces a 60 mm endo-GIA stapler with a blue cartridge into the surgical field.
The surgeon applies the endo-GIA stapler to the jejunal side and then to the gastric side, ensuring complete coverage before locking the stapler in place.
The surgeon assesses the posterior aspect to ensure no omentum or mesentery is encroaching on the staple line before firing the endo-GIA stapler.
The surgeon completes the anterior gastrojejunostomy, leaving the enterotomy visibly open, which requires closure.
The surgeon assesses the staple line for any bleeding and uses suction to remove residue from the surgical area, addressing the complete gastric outlet obstruction.
The surgeon introduces a 2-0 PDS suture and places a stay suture on the right side of the enterotomy edge, preparing for the completion of the anastomosis.

The surgeon cuts the stay suture thread slightly longer and begins continuous closure from the left side of the enterotomy opening. The first suture bite is taken on the gastric side, followed by a reverse bite on the jejunal side.
The surgeon performs the closure of the enterotomy, moving from the stomach to the jejunum, and prepares to place the knot to secure the suture.

The surgeon secures the knot and continues the enterotomy closure, starting with the stomach side and then proceeding to the jejunum side. Multiple reverse bites are taken on the jejunal side to ensure adequate closure.
The surgeon takes full-thickness bites during the closure of the enterotomy, ensuring that the tissue is tightened without any mucosa protruding from the suture line.

The surgeon continues the closure of the enterotomy, utilizing the stay suture to expose the angles for easier access. The stay suture aids in managing the challenging angles, facilitating precise placement of sutures.
The surgeon ensures the closure is tight by taking suturing bites one side at a time, including at the angles of the enterotomy opening.
The surgeon reaches the edge of the closure, extending beyond the stay suture. The knot is placed, completing the closure with one side at a time.
The surgeon completes the crastose adenostomy and performs an air leak test to visually assess the adequacy of the closure, marking the end of the procedure.
